demo_steps.title: Understanding the Tensor Structure
demo_steps.narration: This is a 3D representation of a tensor used in image processing. Notice the three labeled dimensions: height (H), width (W), and channels (C), which are critical in defining a color image.

demo_steps.title: Rotating the Tensor
demo_steps.narration: Use rotation to see how each dimension interacts with the others. As you spin it, notice how the grid lines help define the tensor boundaries in 3D space, making the dimensions clearer.

demo_steps.title: Significance of Tensor Dimensions
demo_steps.narration: Recognize the importance of each axis. Height and width form the spatial resolution, while channels typically correspond to color information. Together, they outline the way data is structured in vision systems.

plan.camera_suggestion: Initial isometric view, clearly showing H, W, C axes with slight elevation for depth perception, matching original image silhouette.

plan.contextChunk:
![The tensors that represent one pass through the MLP in @fig-neural_nets-simple_MLP_network](figures/neural_nets/simple_MLP_network_tensors_and_batches-2.png){#fig-neural_nets-simple_MLP_network_tensors_and_batches width=70%}

This example shows the basic concept of working with tensors and batches
for one-dimensional data, but, in vision, most of the time we will be
working with higher-dimensional tensors. For image data we typically use
four-dimensional tensors: batch $\times$ channels $\times$ height
$\times$ width; for videos we may use five-dimensional tensors: batch
$\times$ channels $\times$ height $\times$ width $\times$ time.
Three-dimensional (3D) scans have an additional *depth* spatial
dimension; videos of 3D data could therefore be represented by
six-dimensional tensors. As you can see, thinking in terms of
two-dimensional matrices is not quite sufficient. Instead, you should be
imagining data processing as operating on $N$-dimensional tensors,
sliced and diced in different ways. As a step in this direction, you may
find it useful to visualize tensors in 3D, as shown in
@fig-neural_nets-3D_tensor_example.


![A 3D tensor that could represent an $C \times H \times W$ color image.](figures/neural_nets/3D_tensor_example.png){#fig-neural_nets-3D_tensor_example width=20%}

This is closer to the actual ND tensors vision systems work with, and
many concepts can be adequately captured just by thinking in 3D. We will
see some examples in later chapters.

## Catalog of Layers

Below, we use the color blue to denote
[**parameters**]{.param_color_dark} and the color red to
denote [**data/activations**]{.data_color_dark} (inputs
and outputs to each layer). 

:::{.column-margin}
We color equations in
this way only in this chapter, to make clear the roles of different

label X positioned on top of the cuboid

interactions.teaches: Allows viewing the tensor from different angles to understand its 3D structure

demo_steps.title: Explore the 3D Tensor
demo_steps.narration: Rotate around the 3D tensor to see how its dimensions are structured, focusing on height H, width W, and channels C. Notice how these dimensions form the basic building blocks of an image tensor.

demo_steps.title: Height, Width, Channels
demo_steps.narration: Observe how each axis of the cube represents a dimension used in data processing: height (H), width (W), and channels (C). These correspond to the properties of an image input to neural networks.

demo_steps.title: Relevance in Vision Systems
demo_steps.narration: Understand that this cube is a simplified model for how vision systems process data, allowing you to conceptualize the transformation from 2D to higher-dimensional tensors.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial view similar to original: isometric angle showing full H, W, and C dimensions prominently
